topic: A neighbor wants to install a fence
raw_output:
[story 1]

The sound of the hammering started early, a jarring intrusion into the quiet morning. I was making coffee, the steam rising in a comforting curl, when the rhythmic thudding began right at the property line. My knuckles went white around the mug handle. I peered through the blinds, and there he was—Mr. Henderson, already in his yard, wielding a post-hole digger with an almost aggressive energy. Every swing seemed to vibrate through the thin wall separating our properties. I stood up too quickly, the chair scraping loudly against the linoleum floor. My heart began to thump a quick, uneven rhythm against my ribs. I needed a moment, a solid anchor. I retreated to the living room, gripping the edge of the sofa until my fingers ached, trying to steady the frantic fluttering in my chest.

[story 2]

"Just a little longer, Martha," Mr. Henderson called out, his voice carrying easily over the hedge. I felt a cold knot tighten in my stomach. I was trying to read, ostensibly, but my eyes kept drifting toward the construction site. The sheer volume of the work was overwhelming; the relentless noise seemed to press in on me. I set the book down with unnecessary force, the pages fluttering shut. I walked to the window and pressed my forehead against the cool glass, trying to draw a deep, slow breath. My breath hitched halfway down my chest. Every time a piece of wood struck the ground, I flinched involuntarily, my shoulders tightening up toward my ears. I couldn't focus on anything else; the entire atmosphere felt charged and unstable.

[story 3]

I kept glancing toward the boundary line, a nervous energy buzzing just beneath my skin. The sound of the saw cutting through the wood was sharp and insistent, cutting through the usual suburban drone. I paced the length of the hallway twice, my steps quick and shallow. When I finally stopped by the door, I could hear Mr. Henderson talking animatedly to someone on his phone, gesturing emphatically with a piece of lumber. My jaw felt tight, clenched so hard I could feel the strain in my molars. I watched his movements, cataloging every sharp turn and forceful placement of tools. A distinct sense of unease washed over me, a feeling that something was about to shift, and I found myself gripping the doorframe, knuckles white against the painted wood, wishing I could simply disappear.
